subject: Chemistry
solution:
Osmotic pressure ($\pi$) = $12 \text{ atm}$

Temperature ($T$) = $300 \text{ K}$

Gas constant ($R$) = $0.08 \text{ L atm K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$

Sodium chloride (NaCl) is an electrolyte that breaks into ions in an aqueous solution:

$$ \text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + \text{Cl}^- $$

Since it is given to assume complete dissociation, one unit of NaCl produces 2 ions.

Therefore, the van 't Hoff factor, $i = 2$.

According to the formula for osmotic pressure:

$$ \pi = iCRT $$

Substitute the given values into the equation:

$$ 12 = 2 \times C \times 0.08 \times 300 $$

$$ 12 = 2 \times 24 \times C $$

$$ 12 = 48 \times C $$

$$ C = \frac{12}{48} $$

$$ C = 0.25 \text{ mol L}^{-1} $$

Molar mass of NaCl = Molar mass of Na + Molar mass of Cl

$$ \text{Molar mass of NaCl} = 23 + 35.5 = 58.5 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

To convert the concentration from moles per liter to grams per liter (strength), multiply the molar concentration ($C$) by the molar mass:

$$ \text{Strength} (\text{g L}^{-1}) = \text{Molar concentration} \times \text{Molar mass} $$

$$ \text{Strength} = 0.25 \text{ mol L}^{-1} \times 58.5 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

$$ \text{Strength} = 14.625 \text{ g L}^{-1} $$

Rounding off to the nearest integer, we get $15$.

The strength of the sodium chloride solution is 15 $\text{g L}^{-1}$.
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Evening Shift

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Moles of solute $A$:

$$n_A=\frac{0.3}{60}=0.005\ \text{mol}$$

Moles of solute $B$:

$$n_B=\frac{0.9}{180}=0.005\ \text{mol}$$

Total moles of solute particles (both are non-electrolytes, so no dissociation):

$$n=n_A+n_B=0.005+0.005=0.01\ \text{mol}$$

Volume of solution (dilute solution, take volume $\approx 100\ \text{mL}=0.1\ \text{L}$):

$$V=0.1\ \text{L}$$

Temperature:

$$T=27^\circ\text{C}=300\ \text{K}$$

Osmotic pressure:

$$\pi=\frac{n}{V}RT=\frac{0.01}{0.1}\times 0.082 \times 300$$

$$\pi=0.1\times 24.6=2.46\ \text{atm}$$

Correct option: A) $2.46\ \text{atm}$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Morning Shift

subject: Chemistry
solution:
For osmotic pressure, we use the NCERT relation:

$$ \pi = C R T = \frac{n}{V}RT $$

where

  • $\pi$ = osmotic pressure

  • $n$ = number of moles of solute

  • $V$ = volume of solution

  • $R = 0.083 \, \text{L bar mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$

  • $T = 300 \, \text{K}$

Protein molar mass is:

$$ M = 50000 \, \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

Now solve each part.

For solution A

Mass of protein $= 1 \, \text{g}$

Moles of protein:

$$ n_A = \frac{1}{50000} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol} $$

Volume:

$$ V_A = 0.5 \, \text{L} $$

So concentration:

$$ C_A = \frac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol L}^{-1} $$

Hence osmotic pressure:

$$ x = \pi_A = C_A RT $$

$$ x = (4 \times 10^{-5})(0.083)(300) $$

First calculate:

$$ 0.083 \times 300 = 24.9 $$

So,

$$ x = 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 24.9 = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar} $$

For solution B

Mass of protein $= 2 \, \text{g}$

Moles:

$$ n_B = \frac{2}{50000} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol} $$

Volume:

$$ V_B = 1 \, \text{L} $$

Concentration:

$$ C_B = \frac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{1} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol L}^{-1} $$

Thus,

$$ y = \pi_B = C_BRT $$

$$ y = (4 \times 10^{-5})(0.083)(300) $$

$$ y = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar} $$

After mixing A and B

Total moles of protein:

$$ n_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 10^{-5} + 4 \times 10^{-5} = 6 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol} $$

Total volume:

$$ V_{\text{total}} = 0.5 + 1 = 1.5 \, \text{L} $$

Concentration after mixing:

$$ C_{\text{mix}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol L}^{-1} $$

Again,

$$ z = \pi_{\text{mix}} = C_{\text{mix}}RT $$

$$ z = (4 \times 10^{-5})(0.083)(300) = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar} $$

So,

$$ x = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar}, \quad y = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar}, \quad z = 9.96 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{bar} $$

Therefore, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option A}} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Evening Shift

subject: Chemistry
options: Blue color is formed on both sides.
Ionic solutes in aqueous solution can pass through semi-permeable membrane.
To cause the reverse flow of solvent during osmosis, external pressure (any value) should be applied to side ' $x$ '.
solution:
(A) A semi-permeable membrane (SPM) allows only solvent molecules (water) to pass through it. It does not allow ions to pass.

So, the solute ions from the two sides cannot come together. Therefore, no blue colour (due to mixing of ions) will appear on either side.

(B) Because ions cannot cross the SPM, only solvent molecules can move from one side to the other depending on osmotic pressure.

(C) A hypotonic solution is the one which has lower osmotic pressure (lower “effective” concentration of solute particles).

We use the NCERT relation for osmotic pressure:

As osmotic pressure $(\pi)=i C R T$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6^{-4} \quad(\because i=5) \\ & \mathrm{FeCl}_3 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{+3}+\mathrm{3Cl^-} \quad(\because i=4) \\ & \text { for side } x: \pi=5 \mathrm{CRT}=0.5 \mathrm{RT} \quad(\because c=0.1 \mathrm{~m}) \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, y: \pi=0.4 R T \quad(\because c=0.1 \mathrm{~m}) \end{aligned} $$

effective concentration of $x=\dot{i} \times C=0.5 \mathrm{M}$

$$ y=i \times C=0.4 \mathrm{~m} $$

Since, effective concentration of $y$ is less than $x$. Hence, $y$ is hypotonic.

(D) In reverse osmosis, pressure is applied on the side having higher osmotic pressure (here, side $x$). But this applied external pressure must be greater than the osmotic pressure difference between the two sides.

i.e. P applied > $\left(\pi_x-\pi_y\right)$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift

subject: Chemistry
solution:
For a solution separated from pure water by a semipermeable membrane, at equilibrium the osmotic pressure is balanced by the hydrostatic pressure.

So,

$$ \pi = h \rho g $$

Given:

  • $h = 80.0 \, \text{mm} = 0.080 \, \text{m}$

  • $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg m}^{-3}$

  • $g = 10 \, \text{m s}^{-2}$

Therefore,

$$ \pi = 0.080 \times 1000 \times 10 $$

$$ \pi = 800 \, \text{Pa} = 0.8 \, \text{kPa} $$

Now, using the osmotic pressure formula:

$$ \pi = CRT $$

where $C = \dfrac{n}{V}$

So,

$$ \pi = \frac{w}{MV}RT $$

Here,

  • $w = 20 \, \text{g}$

  • $M =$ molar mass

  • $V = 1 \, \text{L} = 1 \, \text{dm}^3$

  • $R = 8.3 \, \text{kPa dm}^3 \text{K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$

  • $T = 300 \, \text{K}$

  • $\pi = 0.8 \, \text{kPa}$

Substituting,

$$ 0.8 = \frac{20}{M \times 1}\times 8.3 \times 300 $$

$$ 0.8 = \frac{20 \times 8.3 \times 300}{M} $$

$$ M = \frac{20 \times 8.3 \times 300}{0.8} $$

$$ M = 62250 \, \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

Convert into $\text{kg mol}^{-1}$:

$$ M = 62.25 \, \text{kg mol}^{-1} $$

Nearest integer:

$$ \boxed{62 \, \text{kg mol}^{-1}} $$

subject: Chemistry
options:
Both Statement I and Statement II are true

Both Statement I and Statement II are false

Statement I is true but Statement II is false

Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
In chamber (I), the concentration of solute is calculated as:

$$C_1 = \frac{18/180}{0.1} = 1\, \text{M}$$

In chamber (II), the concentration is given by:

$$C_2 = \frac{30/180}{250/1000} = \frac{2}{3}\, \text{M}$$

Since osmotic pressure depends on concentration and $\pi_1 > \pi_2$, water will move from the side having lower osmotic pressure (chamber II) to the side having higher osmotic pressure (chamber I).

Osmotic pressure is calculated using the formula:

$$\pi = iCRT$$

For potassium sulfate $(\mathrm{K_2SO_4})$, the van’t Hoff factor is $i = 3$.

So,

$$\pi = 3 \times \left(\frac{50 \times 10^{-3}/174}{2}\right) \times 0.083 \times 300$$

On solving,

$$\pi = 0.0107\, \text{bar}$$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
To calculate the osmotic pressure of the solution at the new temperature, we can use the formula:

$ \pi = \text{CRT} $

Since the concentration $ C $ and the gas constant $ R $ remain constant, the ratio of the osmotic pressures at two different temperatures is given by:

$ \frac{\pi_1}{\pi_2} = \frac{T_1}{T_2} $

Thus, we can express the osmotic pressure at the second temperature as:

$ \pi_2 = \frac{\pi_1 \cdot T_2}{T_1} $

Substituting the given values:

$ \pi_2 = \frac{7 \times 10^5 \times 283}{273} $

After calculation, this simplifies to:

$ \pi_2 = 72.56 \times 10^4 \, \text{Nm}^{-2} $
title: JEE Main 2024 (Online) 29th January Morning Shift

subject: Chemistry
solution:
An artificial cell contains a 0.2 M glucose solution within a semipermeable membrane. When this cell is placed in a 0.05 M NaCl solution at 300 K, we need to determine the osmotic pressure developed, expressed as × 10⁻¹ bar.

Given:

Ideal Gas Constant, R = 0.083 L bar mol⁻¹ K⁻¹

Assume complete dissociation of NaCl.

Explanation

The osmotic pressure ($\pi$) is calculated using the formula:

$ \pi = \left( i_1 C_1 - i_2 C_2 \right) R T $

where:

$i_1$ and $C_1$ are the van 't Hoff factor and concentration for glucose.

$i_2$ and $C_2$ are the van 't Hoff factor and concentration for NaCl.

$R$ is the ideal gas constant.

$T$ is the temperature in Kelvin.

For glucose:

$i_1 = 1$ (glucose does not dissociate)

$C_1 = 0.2$ M

For NaCl:

$i_2 = 2$ (NaCl dissociates into two ions, Na⁺ and Cl⁻)

$C_2 = 0.05$ M

Plugging in the values:

$ \pi = \left(1 \times 0.2 - 2 \times 0.05\right) \times 0.083 \times 300 $

Calculating:

$ \pi = (0.2 - 0.1) \times 0.083 \times 300 $

$ \pi = 0.1 \times 0.083 \times 300 $

$ \pi = 2.5 \text{ bar} $

Hence, the osmotic pressure developed is $25 \times 10^{-1}$ bar.

The solute molecules do not cross S.P.M. via osmosis.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The concept we are utilizing to solve this problem is osmotic pressure. Osmotic pressure is the pressure required to stop the flow of solvent into a solution through a semipermeable membrane. For dilute solutions, osmotic pressure behaves similarly to an ideal gas, hence the formula we use is similar to the ideal gas law:

$$\Pi = \frac{n}{V}RT$$

where:

  • $\Pi$ is the osmotic pressure,
  • $n$ is the number of moles of solute,
  • $V$ is the volume of the solution,
  • $R$ is the ideal gas constant, and
  • $T$ is the temperature in Kelvin.

First, we convert all given quantities to the appropriate units.

  • $\Pi = 1.29$ mbar is equivalent to $1.29 \times 10^{-3}$ bar (since 1 bar = 1000 mbar),
  • $V = 300$ cm³ is equivalent to $0.3$ L (since 1 L = 1000 cm³),
  • $T = 300$ K,
  • $R = 0.083$ L bar K$^{-1}$ mol$^{-1}$.

Instead of the number of moles, we are given the mass of the solute. However, we can replace the moles with mass using the relationship $n = \frac{m}{M}$, where $M$ is the molar mass, and $m$ is the mass of the solute.

So the equation becomes:

$$\Pi = \frac{m}{MV}RT$$

We are trying to solve for $M$, so rearrange the equation to isolate $M$:

$$M = \frac{mRT}{\Pi V}$$

Finally, substitute the given values into the equation:

$$M = \frac{0.63 \times 0.083 \times 300}{1.29 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3} \approx 40535 \, \text{g/mol}$$

So the molar mass of the protein is approximately 40535 g/mol.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \pi=\mathrm{M}^{\prime} \mathrm{RT}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{W} / \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{V}}\right) \mathrm{RT} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \ \pi=\left(\frac{\mathrm{W}}{\mathrm{V}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{M}}\right) \mathrm{RT}=\mathrm{C}\left(\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}\right) \\\\ & \Rightarrow \ \frac{\pi}{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}} =\mathrm{Constant}\end{aligned}$

If we assume graph between $\frac{\pi}{\mathrm{C}}$ and $\mathrm{C}$, then right graph should be like this in the question,



$$ \therefore $$ Question's graph is wrong.

Assuming $$\pi $$ vs C graph,

$\begin{aligned} & \pi=C R T \\\\ & \Rightarrow \pi=\frac{\text { mole }}{\text { volume }} \times R T \\\\ & \Rightarrow \pi=\frac{\text { mole }}{\text { volume }} \times \frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{M}} \times \mathrm{RT} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \pi=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }} \times \frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \pi(\mathrm{atm})=\frac{R T}{M} \times C\left(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{lit}^{-1}\right)\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \text {So, Slope }=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}=\frac{0.083 \times 300}{\mathrm{M}}=6 \times 10^{-4} \\\\ & \therefore \mathrm{M}=\frac{0.083 \times 300}{6 \times 10^{-4}}=\frac{830 \times 300}{6} \\\\ & =41,500 \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mole}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
We say that two solutions are isotonic (at the same temperature) if they have the same osmotic pressure, $\pi$. For dilute solutions at the same temperature $T$,

$ \pi = i\, M\, R\, T, $

where

$M$ = molarity of the solution,

$i$ = van 't Hoff factor (number of particles the solute dissociates into),

$R$ = universal gas constant,

$T$ = absolute temperature.

Since $R$ and $T$ are common for both solutions (same temperature), two solutions will be isotonic if

$ i_1 \, M_1 \;=\; i_2 \, M_2. $

Let us check each pair:

1. Pair A: $1\,\mathrm{M}$ NaCl and $2\,\mathrm{M}$ urea

NaCl dissociates into $ \mathrm{Na}^+ $ and $ \mathrm{Cl}^- $.

Hence $i(\mathrm{NaCl}) = 2$.

So $i \, M = 2 \times 1 = 2.$

Urea ($\mathrm{(NH_2)_2CO}$) does not dissociate in water.

Hence $i(\mathrm{urea}) = 1$.

So $i \, M = 1 \times 2 = 2.$

Both solutions have $i M = 2.$

$\therefore$ They are isotonic.

2. Pair B: $1\,\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{CaCl_2}$ and $1.5\,\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{KCl}$

Calcium chloride ($\mathrm{CaCl_2}$) dissociates into $ \mathrm{Ca}^{2+} $ and $ 2\,\mathrm{Cl}^- $.

Hence $i(\mathrm{CaCl_2}) = 3.$

So $i \, M = 3 \times 1 = 3.$

Potassium chloride ($\mathrm{KCl}$) dissociates into $ \mathrm{K}^+ $ and $ \mathrm{Cl}^- $.

Hence $i(\mathrm{KCl}) = 2.$

So $i \, M = 2 \times 1.5 = 3.$

Both solutions have $i M = 3.$

$\therefore$ They are isotonic.

3. Pair C: $1.5\,\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{AlCl_3}$ and $2\,\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{Na_2SO_4}$

Aluminum chloride ($\mathrm{AlCl_3}$) dissociates into $ \mathrm{Al}^{3+} $ and $ 3\,\mathrm{Cl}^- $.

Hence $i(\mathrm{AlCl_3}) = 4.$

So $i \, M = 4 \times 1.5 = 6.$

Sodium sulfate ($\mathrm{Na_2SO_4}$) dissociates into $ 2\,\mathrm{Na}^+ $ and $ \mathrm{SO_4}^{2-} $.

Hence $i(\mathrm{Na_2SO_4}) = 3.$

So $i \, M = 3 \times 2 = 6.$

Both solutions have $i M = 6.$

$\therefore$ They are isotonic.

4. Pair D: $2.5\,\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{KCl}$ and $1\,\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{Al_2(SO_4)_3}$

Potassium chloride ($\mathrm{KCl}$) has $i(\mathrm{KCl}) = 2.$

So $i \, M = 2 \times 2.5 = 5.$

Aluminum sulfate $\mathrm{Al_2(SO_4)_3}$ dissociates into

$ 2\,\mathrm{Al}^{3+} \;+\; 3\,\mathrm{SO_4}^{2-} \quad \Longrightarrow i(\mathrm{Al_2(SO_4)_3}) = 2 + 3 = 5. $

So $i \, M = 5 \times 1 = 5.$

Both solutions have $i M = 5.$

$\therefore$ They are isotonic.

Conclusion

All four given pairs $(A, B, C, D)$ satisfy $i_1 M_1 = i_2 M_2$. Therefore, all of them are isotonic pairs.

Hence, the number of isotonic pairs is:

$ \boxed{4}. $

solution:
Let molar mass of protein A = x g/mol

Let molar mass of protein B = y g/mol

$$\pi $$A = osmotic pressure of protein A = $${{0.73} \over x} \times {{1000} \over {250}} \times RT$$

$$\pi $$B = osmotic pressure of protein B = $${{1.65} \over y} \times {1 \over 1} \times RT$$

Given $$\pi $$A = $$\pi $$B

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${{0.73} \over x} \times {{1000} \over {250}} \times RT$$ = $${{1.65} \over y} \times {1 \over 1} \times RT$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${x \over y}$$ = $${{0.73} \over {0.25 \times 1.65}}$$ = 1.77

= 177 $$ \times $$ 10-2

= $${{0.1 \times 1 + 0.2 \times 2} \over {1 + 2}}$$

= $${{0.5} \over 3}$$ = $${{500} \over 3} \times {10^{ - 3}}$$

solution:
We know, osmotic pressure ($$\pi $$) = (Resultant molarity)$$ \times $$R$$ \times $$T

Resultant molarity = $${{Total\,moles\,of\,all\,solutions} \over {Total\,volume(in\,lit)}}$$
= $${{{{0.6} \over {60}} + {{1.8} \over {180}}} \over {0.1}}$$ = 0.2 mol/lit

$$ \therefore $$ Osmotic pressure ($$\pi $$) = (0.2)$$ \times $$0.08206$$ \times $$300 = 4.92 atm

solution:
TIPS/FORMULAE:

Osmotic pressure $$\left( \pi \right)$$ of isotonic solutions are equal.

For solution of unknown substance $$\left( {\pi = CRT} \right)$$

$${C_1} = {{5.25/M} \over V}$$

For solution of urea,

$$C{}_2\,\,$$ (concentration) $$ = {{1.5/60} \over V}$$

Given, $${\pi _1} = {\pi _2}$$

as $$\,\,\,\,\pi = CRT$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\,\,\,\,\,{C_1}RT = {C_2}RT\,\,$$ or $$\,\,{C_1} = {C_2}$$

or $$\,\,\,\,{{5.25/M} \over V} = {{1.8/60} \over V}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\,\,\,\,\,M = 210g/mol$$
